text: welcome to another video of robotic hernia surgery today we're going to review a basic inguinal hernia repair and try to highlight the importance of doing some straightforward cases minimally invasive inguinal hernia surgery takes a ton of repetition to get good at and perform efficiently i think performing simple cases is essential and will make the hard ones easier so let's review an easy one this is unedited and at around i think two and a half x This case is a demonstration of a straightforward indirect inguinal hernia in a patient without many abdominal procedures.
The surgeon identifies the preperitoneal pockets within the parietal compartment medially during the dissection of a straightforward inguinal hernia.

The surgeon works laterally to access the visceral compartment, addressing minor bleeding on the right side. A thin flap is maintained laterally, and the surgeon identifies a deeper layer to avoid medializing the cord element.

The surgeon enters the visceral compartment to efficiently dissect the peritoneal flap, ensuring protection of the lateral sidewall containing nerves and vascular elements. The dissection continues in the parietal compartment and retropubic space to create a flat area for mesh placement.

The surgeon reduces a small hernia sac and begins parietalizing the cord structures. The focus shifts to establishing the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, ensuring an adequate retropubic dissection with at least two centimeters of space between the bladder and the pubis.
The surgeon dissects beyond the midline, ensuring at least two centimeters of space, and begins examining various hernia spaces, including the femoral, obturator, indirect, and direct spaces.

The surgeon demonstrates parietalization of the vascular structures by reflecting down the peritoneum and grasping the medial umbilical ligament to parietalize the vas. The fatty tissue is differentiated from the femoral lymph nodes. The surgeon then works around the obturator space, creating a clear space for mesh placement.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery and decides not to transect it, as it would not aid in developing an appropriate pocket. The procedure continues with lateral dissection.

The surgeon examines the surgical field to confirm the absence of a cord lipoma, identifies the transversalis sling, and ensures no lipomas are missed. The scissors are exchanged for a mega suture cut instrument to place the mesh and close the flap.

The surgeon places a mid-weight polypropylene mesh into the surgical pocket, securing it with sutures. The mesh is positioned carefully to fit the pocket, ensuring stability and minimizing the risk of movement.

The surgeon assesses the pocket size created for the mesh, noting it is perfectly sized to prevent movement. For small hernias, fixation is deemed non-critical, and the mesh is selectively fixated, with no fixation applied in this case.

The surgeon observes the flap and mesh moving independently, emphasizing the importance of this separation. The flap closure begins from lateral to medial, ensuring the medial aspect, which lacks redundancy, is closed last to prevent a tight lateral closure.
